query: Why must data be a function in Vue components?

competitors.label: Options: State
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical Vue API reference for the data option, including the official rule that component data must be provided by a function returning reactive instance state.
theirStrengths: The official API page is authoritative on what data() returns and how Vue turns the returned object into reactive instance state.
theirGaps: It is less bug-first about shared-reference leaks across component instances and SSR-style cross-request contamination.

realWorldUseCases: each instance gets its own state object.
multiple instances would mutate the same reference and leak state between components.
followUpConfusion: If you want instance state (almost always): use data().
If you want intentionally shared state: don’t “accidentally” share via data: {}

realWorldUseCases: The docs define the API correctly, but our page is more explicit about the concrete leak pattern that appears when sibling instances share the same object.
actionableExamples: The official examples are still solid for the API shape, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on the likely follow-up distinction between intentional shared state via a store and accidental sharing through a plain object in data.

actionableExamples: Add one tiny two-card example later that shows one instance's counter affecting another.

competitors.winReason: The official API reference is authoritative, but our page is stronger for interview use because it frames the rule around instance isolation and the exact bugs shared references create.

competitors.label: Vue Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's Vue interview bank is directly relevant because component instance state, props, and shared-state pitfalls are core Vue interview fundamentals.
theirStrengths: It is a broad interview-prep resource for Vue component-instance behavior and state ownership topics.
theirGaps: The category page is broader than this exact data-per-instance rule and shared-reference failure mode.

followUpConfusion: A function returns a fresh object per instance.

realWorldUseCases: The bank is relevant at the topic level, but it does not make this exact shared-state bug pattern as explicit on the landing page.
actionableExamples: GreatFrontEnd remains competitive as an interview-prep source, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: The category page is less explicit about why root app state can be shared intentionally while reusable component instances cannot.

actionableExamples: Keep the multi-instance state-leak example short and visible so the rule does not collapse into a memorized framework quirk.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is useful as a Vue fundamentals hub, but our page is stronger because it explains the per-instance rule through the exact shared-object bug it prevents.

competitors.label: Props Versus Data in Vue
competitors.selectionReason: Michael Thiessen's article is a strong tutorial reference on component-private data versus parent-owned props, which is directly relevant to instance-local state ownership.
theirStrengths: It is practical on the difference between component-local data and parent-provided props.
theirGaps: It is broader on props versus data and less focused on why reusable component data must be produced by a function.

followUpConfusion: component data must be a function so each instance gets its own state object.
Returning a function is slightly more boilerplate but safer.

realWorldUseCases: The article explains local data ownership well, but our page is stronger on the specific bug class that appears when the same data object is shared across instances.
actionableExamples: Its props-versus-data examples are still useful, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on the likely follow-up that shared state should be made explicit through a store or module instead of sneaking in through data.

actionableExamples: If needed later, add one SSR-specific note explaining why shared component data can leak between requests.

competitors.winReason: This tutorial is helpful on data ownership, but our page is stronger because it turns the rule into a precise per-instance safety argument rather than a broad props-versus-data explanation.
